← the Atlas

arivu — terminal awareness as an ephemeral surface on kaval

feature · seedling ·accepted ·JUN 19, 2026

A clean-slate design for kolu terminal awareness (git · PR · agent · foreground). Repeat the kaval decoupling one level up — the awareness sensors become a generic library kolu runs in-process locally (writing terminalMetadata directly), and an ephemeral daemon (arivu) on top of kaval serves the same awareness over ssh for remote terminals, mirrored + folded into kolu. One sensor library, two homes — in-process locally, a served daemon remotely. It ships standalone first (arivu + arivu-tui, the kaval-tui move one level up).

Contents

  • The core model
  • One library, two homes — and why only remote folds
  • The standalone tool — arivu + arivu-tui
  • Five shippable slices
  • P1 in detail — P1a (extract) + P1b (finish the rename) + P1c (the standalone tool)
    • What you get — user-facing
    • How it’s built — architecture + types
    • The shape on disk
    • How it runs — the process graph
  • Load-bearing complexity & open decisions
  • Status

kaval decoupled the PTY out of kolu into a standalone, minimal, durable daemon with a clean contract — independently shippable, independently driven by kaval-tui. This note does the same move one level up, for the part kolu kept tangling: terminal awareness (git branch/dirty, PR status, agent detection, foreground). A new ephemeral daemon — arivu (Tamil அறிவு, “the faculty of knowing” — a sibling to kaval, watch/guard, and odu, run) — sits on top of kaval, runs the sensors, and exposes its result as one typed @kolu/surface collection. Locally, kolu runs that sensor library in-process and writes terminalMetadata directly — no surface, no boundary. Remotely, the arivu daemon serves the same awareness over ssh, which kolu mirrors and folds in. One sensor library, two homes — and kolu becomes a consumer of awareness, never the place it tangles.

The core model

One thesis: awareness is a derived surface, and the thing that produces it is a standalone daemon on top of kaval. Three claims follow, each verified against the shipped code.

  • The unit is a generic awareness value, not kolu’s record. arivu exposes Collection<TerminalId, AwarenessValue>, where AwarenessValue is exactly the fields the sensors compute — cwd · git · lastAgentCommand · lastActivityAt plus pr · agent · foreground. This schema is generic: it lives in @kolu/terminal-awareness, composed from the vendor-neutral anyagent / anyforge / kolu-git shapes, with no kolu-specific content at all. The one kolu concept — location (the endpoint discriminator) — is simply absent (no sensor authors it; a remote tool can’t know its own kolu-side hostId). Kolu’s own TerminalServerMetadata is then just AwarenessValue plus location and the client/UI fields (themeName, intent, …): the awareness value isn’t carved out of kolu’s record — kolu’s record is built on top of it.
  • kaval stays byte-for-byte minimal. arivu dials kaval as a ptyHostSurface client for the four taps + getScreenText; it adds zero awareness/git/gh logic to kaval. The decoupling is additive — a layer above, never a fork.
  • Ephemerality is the simplifier. Unlike a PTY, awareness never has to survive a restart — it’s re-derivable from live taps + current host fs. So arivu sheds all of kaval’s durability machinery: no single-instance lock, no spawn/cleanup (it owns no PTYs), no persisted list (it borrows kaval’s terminal.list), no adoption or reconnect-survival. Every (re)start just re-runs the sensors and recomputes from now.

The payoff: clients keep reading the one terminalMetadata collection they always did. The awareness surface is an internal seam, not a new thing the browser learns.

One library, two homes — and why only remote folds

Local and remote share the sensor library and one schema — but they are genuinely different paths, not “only the transport.” The surface, and the fold, exist only where awareness crosses a process boundary:

  • Local → the sensor library, in-process (no surface). kolu-server runs the sensors in-process and writes terminalMetadata directly through the existing mutators — no surface, no directLink, no fold. The sensors and the record live in the same process, so there is nothing to serve and nothing to merge; wrapping them in an in-process surface would be the fictional boundary rejected in #1406. Local stays this way for good — the in-process sensors are always the running build, so awareness is current by construction.

Even after remote ships (P2), local does NOT switch to dialing a local arivu “for consistency.” That would trade freshness-by-construction and a plain function call for a separate process, per-delta serialization, and a fold — pure ceremony to move data within one process. The consistency that matters is already there: local and remote share the same library, schema, and AwarenessValue contract; only the transport differs, and it differs because the process boundary is real remotely and absent locally. if local → in-process; else → mirror + fold is the honest shape, with the cheap path on the common case. (A pure-consumer TUI-kolu that runs no sensors itself is the one shape that would dial a local arivu — a different product, not kolu-server.)

  • Remote → serve, mirror, fold. arivu is Nix-provisioned over ssh (the same staleKey recipe kaval already uses), runs the identical sensors, and serves the slice over stdioLink. kolu-server mirrors it with the already-shipped mirrorRemoteCollection, then folds each upsert into the terminal’s terminalMetadata entry.

The mirror is a fold, not a write — and that's structural, not incidental

terminalMetadata’s entry is one mutable object that publishes the whole record — server fields and kolu-owned client fields and the host-stamped location. So an arriving awareness slice can never whole-value-replace the entry (that would clobber the client’s theme/layout and the location). It must be merged in and split across the persisted-vs-live fence: the live subset (pr · agent · foreground) through the quiet mutator, the persisted subset (cwd · git · lastAgentCommand · lastActivityAt) through the one that arms autosave. Lose that split and you either re-arm the ~150 ms agent firehose (a perf regression) or silently stop persisting. This fold is irreducible domain logic — it exists because terminalMetadata is co-owned, and no generic mirror can absorb it.

So the asymmetry is real, and confined to the wire. Local is a closure-mutate straight to terminalMetadata; remote is closure → frame → serve → mirror → fold. They share the invariant sensor library and one schema — but they are two genuinely different paths, and pretending otherwise hides the one new artifact (the host-side hooks impl that reifies each mutate closure into a serialized slice frame — a wire carries values, not closures). location is stamped kolu-side from the dial-list at the fold.

No @kolu/surface change is required. The remote serve + mirror path already ships end-to-end, proven by the remote-process-monitor example (a surface served over stdioLink, mirrored back via mirrorRemoteCollection). A future graduation — mirrorRemoteSurface, the spec-driven dual of implementSurface that drives every primitive of a served surface into a local ctx — is attractive electricity (the demo would delete its hand-rolled bridge loop), but it is not on the critical path and it does not dissolve the fold.

The standalone tool — arivu + arivu-tui

Like kaval, the daemon is a deliverable in its own right, shipped and proven before kolu touches it. Where kaval-tui shows what’s running in each PTY, arivu-tui shows what each terminal is in — a host-local “what is every agent doing, across every repo” dashboard with zero kolu-server dependency, dialable over ssh against a prod box.

arivu-tui · ssh nix@prod
$ arivu-tui list --host prod
ID BRANCH DIRTY PR AGENT FOREGROUND
a3f1 feat/dial-ssh ● #1412 ✓ claude · working node
b7c2 master · — codex · waiting codex
c9d4 fix/fold ● #1408 ✗ — nvim
# derived intelligence over the raw PTY — and a future TUI-kolu consumes the same daemon

It ships self-contained: the awareness sensors (now @kolu/terminal-awareness/sensors.ts (extracted in P1a)) import nothing from kolu-server or kolu-common but a logger, and the host runtime deps are just node · git · gh (SQLite via Node’s built-in node:sqlite — no native addon), so the bin travels cleanly over ssh. Defining the awareness contract once yields its consumers off one schema: the daemon (serves it), the TUI (reads it), and — remotely — kolu’s mirror. Locally, kolu uses the sensor library directly and never touches the surface.

Five shippable slices

Each slice ships and is provable on its own; the long pole is P1a → P1b → P1c → P2. P1a, P1b, and P1c are the three parts of one P1 (the standalone story; detailed below) — extract the library, finish the naming, then build the daemon on top. P1a and P1b are behavior-preserving refactors.

PhaseKindShipsRetires the risk of…
P1a #1413 refactorextract @kolu/terminal-awareness — the awareness sensors + the generic schemas, decoupled from kolu-common; kolu-server + kolu-common re-point at it, and the package’s own surface adopts the sensor · signal · sink vocabulary (startAwareness)the kolu coupling — a clean generic package, behavior-preserving (green CI is the proof; kolu keeps writing terminalMetadata in-process, byte-identical to today)
P1b #1419 refactorfinish the rename in the leaves — provider → adapter across anyagent · anyforge · the agent/forge integration packages (symbols, module + file names, exports): the per-vendor plugin becomes the adapter, finishing the spine P1a half-shipped (FORGE_ADAPTERS still keyed a PrProvider) — zero new vocabulary. The live Watcher handle and the wire provider discriminant staythe cross-package naming overload (“provider” meant several things); one coherent, behavior-preserving rename with its own review, off the awareness critical path
P1c #1428 featurearivu + arivu-tui standalone — the daemon dials kaval and serves the awareness surface; the TUI viewer reads it — zero kolu-server involvementdo the sensors run as a separate, kaval-dialing process and produce correct awareness with no in-process handle? + ships the independently-useful tool, before kolu depends on a line of it
P2featurego remote — Nix-provision arivu over ssh; kolu-server’s remote endpoint mirrors its surface and folds into terminalMetadata; the host hooks-impl reifies closures → tagged frames; a version handshake gates incompatible hosts into re-provisionthe genuinely-new artifacts, confined to the wire — reification, the persisted/live fence across the link, version-skew, the cross-arch Nix
P3refactor(optional) graduate mirrorRemoteSurface as the spec-driven dual of implementSurface; the awareness mirror and remote-process-monitor both consume itper-surface hand-mirroring for every future served surface — pure decomplecting, strictly additive, off the critical path

P1 in detail — P1a (extract) + P1b (finish the rename) + P1c (the standalone tool)

P1 has three parts. P1a extracts @kolu/terminal-awareness (the sensor library) and re-points kolu-server + kolu-common at it, and renames the package’s own surface to the sensor · signal · sink vocabulary — a behavior-preserving refactor: kolu keeps running the sensors in-process and writing terminalMetadata exactly as today; only where the code lives (and what the symbols are called) changes (one shared copy, never a duplicate). P1b finishes that rename through the anyagent/anyforge leaves — provider → adapter, symbols and module names alike (a design panel rejected a second probe spine; the live handle stays the watcher, and the wire provider discriminant is left untouched) — so the whole surface speaks one vocabulary (also behavior-preserving). P1c builds the two binaries on top of that library — arivu (the daemon) and arivu-tui (the viewer) — needing no kolu-server at runtime, exactly the way kaval did. The local story is complete at P1a — nothing kolu-server does ever changes for local terminals; the daemon and the wire are P1c and P2. Two binaries fall out of one contract.

What you get — user-facing

Two commands, mirroring kaval / kaval-tui:

  • arivu — the daemon. Point it at a kaval socket and it serves live awareness for every terminal that kaval owns: 
    arivu --kaval /run/kaval.sock           # serve on a local socket
arivu --kaval /run/kaval.sock --stdio   # serve over stdio (what an ssh dial speaks to)
  • arivu-tui — the viewer. Dial a local arivu, or one across ssh: 
    arivu-tui list                # the dashboard — every terminal, one row each
arivu-tui list --host prod    # … the same, on a remote box, over ssh
arivu-tui watch a3f1          # follow one terminal live

The experience is the arivu-tui mockup above: per terminal — repo + branch, dirty or clean, the open PR and its checks, which AI agent and whether it’s working or waiting on you, the foreground process — updating live. Zero kolu-server, zero browser; the runtime is just node · git · gh. It’s a “what is every agent doing, across every repo” pane you can ssh into — useful the day it ships, before kolu depends on a line of it.

How it’s built — architecture + types

One contract, defined once and imported by every consumer. The value type is generic — owned by the package that produces it, never by kolu:

// @kolu/terminal-awareness — the GENERIC awareness value, owned where it's produced.
// Composed from vendor-neutral shapes (anyagent · anyforge · kolu-git); imports NO kolu-common.
export const TerminalIdSchema = z.string().uuid();
export const AwarenessValueSchema = z.object({
  cwd, git,                          // GitInfo (kolu-git)
  lastAgentCommand, lastActivityAt,
  pr,                                // PrResult (anyforge)
  agent,                             // AgentInfo (anyagent: claude · codex · opencode)
  foreground,
}); // zero kolu-specific content — no `location`, no client/UI fields

// kolu-common no longer OWNS these — it EXTENDS the generic base:
//   TerminalServerMetadataSchema = AwarenessValueSchema.merge({ location: HostLocationSchema })

// @kolu/arivu-contract — wraps the value in the one surface every consumer shares
import { defineSurface, type SurfaceTypes } from "@kolu/surface/define";
export const arivuSurface = defineSurface({
  cells:       { version: { schema: VersionSchema, default: DEFAULT_VERSION } }, // the remote (P2) contract-version handshake
  collections: { awareness: { keySchema: TerminalIdSchema, schema: AwarenessValueSchema } },
});
export type AwarenessValue =
  SurfaceTypes<typeof arivuSurface.spec>["collections"]["awareness"]["Value"];

The daemon dials kaval as a plain PtyHostClient — kaval is untouched, arivu is just another client of it, like kaval-tui — and for each terminal in kaval’s terminal.list runs the existing, unchanged sensors:

import {
  startAwareness,
  type AwarenessRecord, type AwarenessSignals, type AwarenessSink,
} from "@kolu/terminal-awareness"; // the sensor set, lifted out of kolu-server verbatim

function watchTerminal(id: TerminalId, kaval: PtyHostClient, ctx: ArivuCtx): () => void {
  const record: AwarenessRecord = { pid, meta: seedAwareness(cwd), currentAgent: null };
  const channels: AwarenessSignals = bridgeKavalTaps(kaval, id); // cwd·title·commandRun·foreground (git made internally)
  const publish = (m: AwarenessValue) => ctx.collections.awareness.upsert(id, m); // generic value — no location to strip
  const hooks: AwarenessSink = {
    // THE one seam that differs from kolu-server: instead of mutating kolu's entry.meta,
    // mutate this record's meta, then publish the (location-less) slice into the served collection.
    updateServerMetadata:     (r, mutate) => { mutate(r.meta); publish(r.meta); },
    updateServerLiveMetadata: (r, mutate) => { mutate(r.meta); publish(r.meta); },
    readScreenText: (n) => kaval.surface.terminal.getScreenText({ id, tailLines: n }),
  };
  return startAwareness(record, id, channels, hooks, ctx.log); // logger injected — the one coupling severed in the lift-out
}

It then serves that surface — serveOverStdio({ router, transport }) for the --stdio/ssh case, a local socket otherwise. arivu-tui is a thin surface client: it dials the daemon, reads awareness (snapshot-then-delta, per the framework), and renders rows.

Two things to notice, because they are exactly what P1 proves:

  • The reuse seam is the sensor set, not the hooks signature — and it’s a move, not a copy. startAwareness / AwarenessRecord / AwarenessSignals / AwarenessSink (and the kaval-tap→channel bridge) relocate from kolu-server into @kolu/terminal-awareness — the only coupling to sever is the logger, injected as a param — and kolu-server re-points its own import at the package in the same change (behavior-preserving: it keeps running the sensors in-process). So there is one copy of the freshness-critical sensor computation, imported by both; P1 adds no duplicate. The only per-consumer code is the thin sink adapter — a AwarenessSink impl: publish-into-a-collection in arivu vs mutate-kolu’s-entry.meta in kolu-server. That differing impl is the variation point the interface exists for, not duplication — and it is exactly what P2 reuses on the far side of the ssh link.
  • No fold here — and none locally, ever. The daemon owns the whole AwarenessValue — no co-owned terminalMetadata, no client fields, no location. The fold (merge + persisted/live split) is kolu-side and remote-only: it first appears in P2, where awareness crosses the wire. Locally kolu never folds — it writes terminalMetadata directly. P1c is the clean, fold-free proof that the sensors run correctly as a separate, kaval-dialing process — the single biggest unknown, retired in isolation.

The shape on disk

Three new packages plus behavior-preserving re-points — split across P1a (the library + the re-points) and P1c (the daemon + viewer); the [P1a]/[P1c] tags mark which. (←move/←del/←edit mark what happens to existing files; everything else is new.)

packages/
  arivu-contract/        NEW [P1c]  the awareness surface — served by the daemon, read by the TUI + remote kolu
    src/index.ts                   arivuSurface = defineSurface({ awareness, version }); imports AwarenessValue from terminal-awareness
    package.json · tsconfig.json
  terminal-awareness/    NEW [P1a]  the sensor set + the generic awareness schemas — decoupled from kolu-common
    src/sensors.ts           ←move from server's terminalEndpoint/providers.ts, then renamed (sensor · signal · sink vocab)
    src/agentRecency.ts      ←move
    src/schema.ts                  AwarenessValue (persisted/live halves) + AgentInfo/PrResult/Foreground/TerminalId — moved OUT of kolu-common
    src/kavalChannels.ts     [P1c]  a PtyHostClient's taps -> AwarenessSignals (the kaval-dial bridge; only the daemon needs it — kolu-server builds its own channels in local.ts)
    src/*.test.ts            ←move agentRecency.test.ts rides along
    src/index.ts · package.json · tsconfig.json
  arivu/                 NEW [P1c]  the daemon  (bin: arivu)
    src/daemon.ts                  per terminal: dial kaval · run startAwareness · publish the slice
    src/hooks.ts                   the AwarenessSink impl -> awareness collection (the one differing seam)
    src/bin.ts                     CLI: arivu --kaval <sock> [--stdio]  ·  serveOverStdio | local socket
    package.json · tsconfig.json
  arivu-tui/             NEW [P1c]  the viewer  (bin: arivu-tui)
    src/bin.ts                     CLI: list [--host] · watch <id>
    src/render.ts                  dial arivu, read `awareness` (snapshot+delta), draw rows
    package.json · tsconfig.json
  server/                MOD [P1a] (behavior-preserving)
    src/terminalEndpoint/local.ts        ←edit import startAwareness from @kolu/terminal-awareness; pass logger
    src/terminalEndpoint/providers.ts    ←del  (moved out)
    src/terminalEndpoint/agentRecency.ts ←del  (moved out)
    package.json                         ←edit + dep @kolu/terminal-awareness
  common/                MOD [P1a] (the schema home inverts — leaves no kolu coupling in terminal-awareness)
    src/surface.ts                       ←edit AwarenessValue + AgentInfo/PrResult/Foreground move to @kolu/terminal-awareness;
                                              re-export them; TerminalServerMetadata = AwarenessValue + location
default.nix              ←edit [P1c] register arivu + arivu-tui as build outputs (full ssh provisioning is P2)
pnpm-workspace.yaml · pnpm-lock.yaml  ←edit the new packages

And how they depend on each other — what arivu pulls in, and the single new edge into existing code:

Deps & shape: the lifted package imports nothing from kolu-server or kolu-common (its lone runtime coupling — the logger — becomes a param), depending only on generic tools (anyagent · anyforge · kolu-git/github · kaval · @kolu/surface); the daemon adds only the surface runtime + a PtyHostClient; agent-session reads use Node’s built-in node:sqlite (no native addon). So arivu provisions and travels over ssh by the same staleKey recipe kaval uses — which is the whole reason to prove it here, before P2 introduces the wire.

How it runs — the process graph

The package graph above is build-time; this is runtime. The honest P1 picture has kolu running, unchanged — and arivu standing up alongside it, off the same substrate, without being wired in yet:

  • kolu keeps working — untouched. In P1, kolu-server still dials kaval for PTYs and runs the sensors in-process for local terminals, writing terminalMetadata exactly as today. P1 changes none of that behavior — only where the sensor code lives (the package move).
  • arivu runs in parallel, off the same substrate. It dials the same kaval and reads the same host fs, running the same sensor code as a standalone process — but it serves its own surface and writes nothing of kolu’s. The two derive awareness independently and never touch. That isolation is the point: prove the sensors run standalone with zero risk to the running product.
  • kolu wires it in only for remote — never locally. The dashed edge is P2 (remote): kolu-server dials a remote arivu over ssh, then mirrors + folds. There is no local wiring — locally kolu keeps running the sensor library in-process and writing terminalMetadata directly (the whole local story, done at P1a). In P1 the dashed edge doesn’t exist yet: arivu + arivu-tui are a closed standalone loop next to the untouched product.

Load-bearing complexity & open decisions

The cleanest design still carries four irreducible costs — all remote-only, none dissolvable, and that confinement is the point:

  1. Closure → frame reification — the one genuinely-new artifact: the host hooks-impl applies each mutate to its own record, then emits the resulting slice value as a snapshot/delta frame. The sensors are untouched.
  2. The fold + persisted/live fence, re-applied kolu-side on every remote upsert (see the callout). Frames must carry the split, or it degrades to runtime discipline.
  3. Freshness / semantic version-skew — the deepest. In-process local sensors are always current; a remote host runs a provisioned, possibly-stale binary. A version handshake catches shape skew, not semantic drift (a new agent state an old parser never emits). Bounded by re-provision-on-dial + ephemerality, but only re-provisioning truly closes it — which is why local must never route through the daemon.
  4. location stamped kolu-side at the fold — a small permanent special-case at the seam.

(Cold-start recompute storms and a two-hop key-set lag are operational, not architectural — bounded by idle-gating and the existing pr:pending / agent:null seed.)

Open decisions (defaults in bold): the persisted/live split is carried by a static field→half map applied kolu-side (thinner wire) vs per-frame tags; one arivu per host (mirrors kaval’s per-host socket, maximizes the dashboard) vs per-session; staleKey-gated re-provision (kaval’s existing behavior) vs always-on-dial for the tightest semantic currency; build mirrorRemoteSurface when a second served-surface consumer appears vs now.

Status

Proposed — a clean-slate replacement for the awareness layer, child of kaval-sessions. It supersedes the earlier from-scratch plan ( #1398 , closed) and the in-process provider extraction ( #1409 , closed): both kept awareness inside kolu and split hairs over an in-process compose-vs-serve boundary that this design dissolves by moving the producer into a daemon. Built from an adversarial, cost-blind design panel (verdict: sound, with the asymmetry-is-encapsulated and mirror-is-a-fold corrections folded in above), grounded by reading the shipped code: the in-server sensor set (now @kolu/terminal-awareness/sensors.ts), mirrorRemoteCollection, the remote-process-monitor “define once, implement twice” demo, and kaval’s provisioning recipe. The substrate it stands on — kaval (untouched), location as a first-class discriminated union — is already merged. The long pole is P1a → P1b → P1c → P2, and P1a (the behavior-preserving extraction) is the recommended first move, then P1b (finish the leaf rename), then P1c (the standalone arivu + arivu-tui): independently useful, and it retires the biggest risk before kolu depends on a line of it. There is no local-compose phase — locally kolu runs the sensor library in-process (settled at P1a); the surface and the fold are remote-only (P2).

P1a shipped as #1413 — @kolu/terminal-awareness is extracted, the schema home inverted, kolu-server re-pointed at it, and the package’s own surface renamed to the sensor · signal · sink vocabulary (startProviders → startAwareness), behaviour-identical. (The kavalChannels.ts kaval-dial bridge listed above moved to P1c: it is the daemon’s connective tissue — kolu-server builds its sensor signals from its own pty-host taps in local.ts, so P1a never needed it.) P1b shipped as #1419 — it finished the provider → adapter rename through the anyagent/anyforge leaves (a single adapter spine, no probe; the Watcher handle and the wire provider discriminant stayed), behaviour-preserving. P1c shipped ( #1428 , merged 2026-06-19) — the standalone arivu (the daemon) + arivu-tui (the viewer), built on the lifted sensor set: arivu dials kaval as a plain ptyHostSurface client, runs the unchanged sensors per terminal, and serves each slice into the @kolu/arivu-contract awareness collection; ephemeral (no gate, no PTY ownership), zero kolu-server at runtime. --stdio is the seam P2’s ssh dial will speak to. Next: P2, the remote mirror + fold.

← the Atlas github ↗
